title: Laptop & Mobile Hardware: FRUs, Displays, Docking & Device Types
summary: Covers laptop field-replaceable units, display technologies (LCD/OLED, backlight, digitizer), function keys and hardware switches, docking vs port replicators, mobile device categories, and components unique to mobile devices.
content:
Laptops are built around field-replaceable units (FRUs) — modular parts a technician can swap without replacing the whole machine, which matters when an embassy IT officer must repair equipment in the field rather than ship it home. The most common FRUs are memory (SO-DIMM), storage (M.2 or 2.5" SATA), the battery, the keyboard, the wireless (WLAN/WWAN) card, and the palmrest assembly. Before any internal service you must power off, unplug AC, remove the battery if it is external/removable, and use ESD precautions (anti-static strap and mat) because laptop boards are dense and static-sensitive.

Laptop memory uses SO-DIMM (Small Outline DIMM), a physically shorter module than the desktop DIMM. DDR4 SO-DIMM has 260 pins and DDR3 SO-DIMM has 204 pins; the keying notch is in a different position so you cannot insert the wrong generation. Modules install at roughly a 30-45 degree angle into the slot, then press down flat until the two side clips snap and lock. Many ultrabooks now solder RAM to the board (non-upgradeable), so always verify before promising a memory upgrade. Match speed/capacity and prefer matched pairs to enable dual-channel.

Storage in modern laptops is dominated by M.2 — a slot/form-factor (e.g., 2280 = 22mm x 80mm) that carries either a SATA M.2 SSD or, far faster, an NVMe M.2 SSD that runs over PCIe. M.2 uses keying notches (B key, M key, B+M) to indicate which interface a slot supports; an NVMe (M-key) drive will not seat in a SATA-only B-key slot. Older or budget laptops still use a 2.5" SATA drive (7mm or 9.5mm height) in a removable caddy. The wireless card is also commonly an M.2 (A/E-key) module with two tiny antenna leads (the gold/black u.FL connectors that route to antennas in the display bezel).

The battery is a lithium-ion or lithium-polymer pack. Symptoms of failure include short runtime, sudden shutdowns, and a swollen/bulging battery — a swollen battery is a safety hazard (fire/expansion risk) and must be removed and disposed of properly, never punctured. The keyboard and palmrest (which houses the touchpad and often the fingerprint reader/power button) are frequently a single assembly connected by a fragile ribbon cable; releasing the ZIF connector latch before pulling the ribbon prevents tearing the contacts.

Display technology is heavily tested. The dominant panel is the LCD, which is not self-lit and therefore needs a backlight. Older LCDs used a CCFL backlight driven by an inverter (which converts DC to the high-voltage AC the lamp needs); a failed inverter or CCFL shows as a very dim image that is only visible with a flashlight at an angle. Modern panels use LED backlights, which need no inverter, run cooler, and dim by varying LED power. LCD sub-types differ by their liquid-crystal arrangement: TN (Twisted Nematic) is cheapest and fastest (low input lag, good for gaming) but has poor viewing angles and weaker color; IPS (In-Plane Switching) has the best color accuracy and widest viewing angles (best for design/diplomatic presentation work) at higher cost; VA (Vertical Alignment) sits in between with the deepest contrast/blacks but slower response.

OLED is a separate technology where each pixel is its own light source (organic LED), so there is NO backlight — black pixels are simply off, yielding perfect blacks, infinite contrast, and very thin panels, at the cost of higher price and potential burn-in. A touchscreen adds a digitizer layer that translates touch (or pen) coordinates into input; the most common is capacitive (senses the conductivity of a finger), versus resistive (pressure-based, works with any stylus). A 2-in-1 or pen-enabled laptop pairs the LCD/OLED with an active digitizer for stylus input.

Laptops integrate components into the display lid: the webcam and microphone array sit in the top bezel, and the Wi-Fi/Bluetooth/WWAN antennas run up the sides of the lid (which is why a cracked hinge can degrade wireless). Privacy-conscious posts often use a physical webcam shutter or kill switch. The exam expects you to know laptop special function keys, accessed via the Fn key combined with the function-row keys (often shown as blue/colored icons): toggle Wi-Fi/airplane mode, adjust screen brightness, control volume/mute, toggle the touchpad, enable keyboard backlight, and switch/extend the display to an external monitor (the "dual displays" or "project" key, mirroring Windows Win+P). Some laptops also have true physical hardware switches/sliders for Wi-Fi/airplane mode.

For expanding a laptop at a desk, distinguish a docking station from a port replicator. A port replicator simply duplicates the laptop's existing ports (USB, video, Ethernet) for quick connect/disconnect. A docking station is more capable: it can add functionality the laptop lacks, supply power, drive multiple external displays, and often connects over a high-bandwidth interface like Thunderbolt or USB-C, sometimes via a proprietary dock connector. Both let an officer arrive, drop the laptop on the dock, and instantly regain keyboard, mouse, wired LAN, and large monitors.

Beyond laptops, the exam covers mobile device categories. Smartphones are pocket cellular computers; tablets are larger touchscreen devices without a built-in keyboard; 2-in-1s (convertibles/detachables) bridge laptop and tablet with a removable or fold-back keyboard; wearables include smartwatches and fitness monitors that pair to a phone (often over Bluetooth/BLE) and may include GPS, heart-rate, and NFC payment; e-readers use low-power e-ink displays optimized for text and long battery life. Other mobile peripherals include VR/AR headsets and GPS units.

Components unique to mobile devices reflect extreme integration. Phones and tablets use an SoC (System on a Chip) that combines CPU, GPU, memory controller, and often the cellular modem on a single die for power efficiency and small size. Their batteries are typically non-replaceable (glued/sealed inside), so battery service means a careful teardown rather than a quick swap. Storage and RAM are usually soldered (non-upgradeable), input is by capacitive touchscreen, and many components (cameras, biometric sensors, modems) are integrated and not user-serviceable. Laptops also offer pointing options beyond the touchpad — the trackpoint (the small pointing stick in the middle of the keyboard, common on ThinkPads) lets a user move the cursor without lifting their hands from the home row.
keyPoints: Common laptop FRUs: SO-DIMM RAM, M.2/2.5" storage, battery, keyboard, WLAN card, palmrest
DDR4 SO-DIMM = 260 pins; DDR3 SO-DIMM = 204 pins; insert at an angle then snap down
M.2 form factor (e.g., 2280) carries SATA or faster NVMe (PCIe) SSDs; keying B/M/B+M
TN = cheap/fast/poor angles; IPS = best color & viewing angles; VA = best contrast/blacks
LCD needs a backlight; CCFL backlights need an inverter (DC->AC); LED backlights do not
OLED = per-pixel self-emitting, no backlight, perfect blacks, risk of burn-in
Digitizer/touchscreen: capacitive (finger conductivity) vs resistive (pressure)
Fn key + function row toggles Wi-Fi/airplane, brightness, volume, touchpad, dual displays
Port replicator duplicates existing ports; docking station adds capability/power/displays
Mobile device types: smartphone, tablet, 2-in-1, wearable, e-reader (e-ink)
Mobile-unique: SoC integrates CPU/GPU/modem; batteries non-replaceable; RAM/storage soldered
Swollen lithium battery is a fire hazard — remove, never puncture, dispose properly

flashcards.front: What does FRU stand for, and give 3 laptop examples
flashcards.back: Field-Replaceable Unit — e.g., SO-DIMM RAM, battery, keyboard, M.2 SSD, WLAN card, palmrest

flashcards.front: Pin counts: DDR4 vs DDR3 SO-DIMM
flashcards.back: DDR4 SO-DIMM = 260 pins; DDR3 SO-DIMM = 204 pins

flashcards.front: What does M.2 '2280' mean?
flashcards.back: Form factor 22mm wide x 80mm long (an M.2 SSD size)

flashcards.front: SATA M.2 vs NVMe M.2 SSD
flashcards.back: SATA M.2 runs over the SATA bus (slower); NVMe M.2 runs over PCIe (much faster)

flashcards.front: TN vs IPS vs VA LCD panels
flashcards.back: TN: cheap, fast, poor angles; IPS: best color/angles; VA: best contrast/blacks, slower

flashcards.front: What does an LCD inverter do?
flashcards.back: Converts DC to high-voltage AC for a CCFL backlight; LED backlights need no inverter

flashcards.front: Symptom of a failed inverter or backlight
flashcards.back: Very dim screen; image only visible with a flashlight at an angle

flashcards.front: Key difference between OLED and LCD
flashcards.back: OLED pixels emit their own light (no backlight), giving perfect blacks; risk of burn-in

flashcards.front: Capacitive vs resistive touchscreen
flashcards.back: Capacitive senses finger conductivity (multi-touch); resistive senses pressure (any stylus)

flashcards.front: Port replicator vs docking station
flashcards.back: Replicator duplicates existing ports; dock adds capability, power, and multiple displays (often Thunderbolt/USB-C)

flashcards.front: Windows shortcut to switch/extend to an external display
flashcards.back: Win+P (Project); laptops also map this to an Fn function key

flashcards.front: What is an SoC in a mobile device?
flashcards.back: System on a Chip — CPU, GPU, memory controller, and often the cellular modem on one die

flashcards.front: Why can't most phone batteries be swapped easily?
flashcards.back: They are non-replaceable: glued/sealed inside, requiring a full teardown to service

flashcards.front: What is a trackpoint?
flashcards.back: The pointing stick in the middle of the keyboard (e.g., ThinkPad) for cursor control without leaving the home row

flashcards.front: What does a swollen laptop battery indicate, and the action?
flashcards.back: Lithium failure / fire hazard — remove it, never puncture, and dispose of it properly

flashcards.front: Where are a laptop's Wi-Fi antennas usually located?
flashcards.back: Routed up the sides of the display lid (bezel); a damaged hinge can hurt wireless

flashcards.front: What is an e-reader's display technology?
flashcards.back: E-ink (electronic paper) — low power, sunlight-readable, optimized for text/long battery life

title: Mobile Connectivity, Configuration, Account Setup, Security & Management
summary: Covers wireless connectivity (Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, NFC, cellular 3G/4G/5G, hotspot, GPS), mobile email/account ports (IMAP/POP3/SMTP/Exchange), synchronization, mobile security and MDM/MAM, and basic mobile troubleshooting.
content:
Mobile devices connect through several wireless radios, and an officer must know each. Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11) is short-range LAN networking: 802.11n (Wi-Fi 4, 2.4/5 GHz), 802.11ac (Wi-Fi 5, 5 GHz), 802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6/6E, adding 6 GHz), and the newest Wi-Fi 7 (802.11be). The 2.4 GHz band reaches farther and penetrates walls better but is slower and crowded; 5/6 GHz are faster with more non-overlapping channels but shorter range. Bluetooth is a short-range (roughly 10 m for Class 2) personal-area-network radio for peripherals (headsets, keyboards, car kits); pairing involves making a device discoverable, selecting it, and confirming a PIN/passkey or a numeric-comparison code so the two devices establish a trusted link. Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) is used by wearables and beacons for minimal power draw.

NFC (Near-Field Communication) is an extremely short-range (about 4 cm) radio used for contactless payments (Apple Pay, Google Pay), badge/access tags, and tap-to-pair. Its tiny range is itself a security feature. Cellular generations progress in speed: 3G (up to a few Mbps), 4G LTE (tens to ~100+ Mbps and the move to all-IP data), and 5G, which adds much higher throughput, low latency, and high device density across low-band (wide coverage), mid-band (balance), and high-band mmWave (very fast but short range, blocked by walls). A mobile hotspot shares the device's cellular data with other devices over Wi-Fi (tethering can also be done over USB or Bluetooth). GPS/location services use satellite trilateration (assisted by cell/Wi-Fi positioning indoors) for maps and geotagging; turning location off, like enabling airplane mode (which disables all radios — cellular, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth — though Wi-Fi/BT can be re-enabled individually), is a common privacy/operational-security measure in sensitive postings.

Setting up mobile email requires knowing the protocols and ports, a classic exam topic. For incoming mail you choose IMAP or POP3. IMAP (Internet Message Access Protocol) keeps mail on the server and syncs folders/read-state across all devices — the right choice for someone using a phone, laptop, and tablet. POP3 (Post Office Protocol v3) downloads mail to one device and traditionally deletes it from the server — single-device, older model. Memorize the ports: IMAP = 143 (unencrypted) / 993 (IMAP over SSL/TLS); POP3 = 110 (unencrypted) / 995 (POP3 over SSL/TLS). Outgoing mail uses SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol): port 25 (server-to-server, often blocked for clients), 465 (implicit SSL), and 587 (submission with STARTTLS — the modern client send port). Always prefer the encrypted ports (993/995/587/465) for diplomatic correspondence.

Corporate/embassy mail commonly uses Microsoft Exchange via ActiveSync (EAS), which pushes email, calendar, and contacts together and lets administrators enforce security policy (require a passcode, encryption, and remote wipe). Modern setup increasingly uses OAuth — a token-based authorization standard so the mail app gets a revocable access token instead of storing the user's actual password (better for MFA and central revocation). For message confidentiality and sender verification, S/MIME uses certificates to digitally sign (proving authenticity/integrity) and encrypt email — important for classified or sensitive State Department communications.

Synchronization keeps a user's data consistent across endpoints. Mobile devices sync to the cloud (iCloud, Google account, Microsoft 365) so contacts, calendar, photos, mail, app data, and settings are available everywhere and survive a lost device; they can also sync to a desktop (via a cable or local software) or sync to an automobile (contacts and media over Bluetooth/Android Auto/CarPlay). Know what data typically syncs: contacts, calendar, email, photos/media, documents, app data, bookmarks, and passwords. Mutual authentication and adequate bandwidth are requirements for reliable sync.

Mobile security starts with the screen lock. Options, roughly in increasing strength/convenience trade-off: swipe (no real security), PIN, password, pattern, and biometrics — fingerprint and facial recognition (e.g., Face ID). Biometrics are convenient and resist shoulder-surfing but can be spoofed and are not legally identical to a memorized passcode; high-security posts often combine a strong passcode with biometrics. Devices should enforce auto-lock timeouts and a failed-attempt lockout/wipe. Full-device encryption (data-at-rest encryption, on by default in modern iOS/Android) ensures a lost or stolen phone's storage is unreadable without the credential — essential for diplomatic data.

Managing a fleet of devices is done with MDM and MAM. MDM (Mobile Device Management) controls the whole device: it enrolls devices, pushes configuration/policy, deploys apps, enforces passcodes/encryption, and can locate, lock, or remote-wipe a lost or compromised device. MAM (Mobile Application Management) is narrower — it manages and secures only the corporate apps and their data (containerization), which suits BYOD because personal data is left alone. Two ownership models: BYOD (Bring Your Own Device) — employee-owned, cheaper but harder to secure and a privacy/legal balance — versus COPE (Corporate-Owned, Personally Enabled) — organization-owned devices that allow some personal use while giving IT full control (preferred where security is paramount, like an embassy). A core management capability is remote wipe (full device or selective corporate-data wipe) to protect data when a device is lost or an employee departs.

App source and sideloading is a key risk topic. The safest software comes from the official app stores (Apple App Store, Google Play), which vet and sign apps. Sideloading (installing apps from outside the store, e.g., an APK) and jailbreaking/rooting (removing the OS sandbox restrictions) bypass these protections and dramatically increase malware risk — they are typically prohibited by embassy policy and detected/blocked by MDM. Always review app permissions (location, camera, microphone, contacts) and revoke what an app does not need.

Mobile troubleshooting follows recurring patterns. For poor battery life or overheating: check for runaway/background apps, high screen brightness, a hot environment or case, a failing battery, or charging while running heavy tasks; close apps, reduce brightness, remove the case to cool, and update the OS. For connectivity problems: toggle airplane mode on/off (forces radios to re-register), confirm you are not in airplane mode, forget and rejoin the Wi-Fi network, re-pair Bluetooth devices, check the SIM/cellular coverage, and reboot. For performance/responsiveness issues: close apps, free up storage, clear app cache, install OS updates, and reboot; a soft reset (restart) fixes many transient faults, while a factory reset is the last resort after backing up. Always escalate device loss with a remote-lock/locate then remote-wipe when recovery is hopeless — protecting the data outranks the hardware.
keyPoints: Wi-Fi: 802.11n=Wi-Fi 4, ac=Wi-Fi 5, ax=Wi-Fi 6/6E; 2.4GHz=range, 5/6GHz=speed
Bluetooth = short-range PAN (~10m) requiring pairing (PIN/passkey); BLE for wearables
NFC ~4cm for contactless pay/badges/tap-to-pair; cellular 3G < 4G LTE < 5G (mmWave fastest, shortest)
IMAP=143/993 (server-synced, multi-device); POP3=110/995 (downloads to one device)
SMTP outgoing = 25 (server), 465 (SSL), 587 (submission/STARTTLS); prefer encrypted ports
Exchange ActiveSync syncs mail/calendar/contacts + enforces policy; OAuth = token auth; S/MIME signs+encrypts
Sync targets: cloud, desktop, automobile; data: contacts, calendar, mail, photos, app data
Screen locks: swipe < PIN/pattern/password < biometrics (fingerprint, face)
Full-device encryption protects data-at-rest on a lost/stolen device
MDM manages the whole device; MAM manages only corporate apps (good for BYOD)
BYOD (employee-owned) vs COPE (corporate-owned, personally enabled); remote wipe protects data
Sideloading/jailbreaking/rooting bypass store vetting and raise malware risk; airplane mode disables all radios

flashcards.front: Wi-Fi generation names: 802.11n / ac / ax
flashcards.back: n = Wi-Fi 4; ac = Wi-Fi 5; ax = Wi-Fi 6 (and 6E adds 6 GHz)

flashcards.front: 2.4 GHz vs 5 GHz trade-off
flashcards.back: 2.4 GHz = longer range, better wall penetration, slower/crowded; 5 GHz = faster, shorter range

flashcards.front: Typical NFC range and uses
flashcards.back: ~4 cm; contactless payments, access badges, tap-to-pair

flashcards.front: Cellular generations from slowest to fastest
flashcards.back: 3G < 4G LTE < 5G; 5G mmWave (high-band) is fastest but shortest range

flashcards.front: IMAP vs POP3 (behavior)
flashcards.back: IMAP keeps mail on the server and syncs across devices; POP3 downloads to one device (often deletes from server)

flashcards.front: IMAP ports
flashcards.back: 143 (unencrypted), 993 (IMAP over SSL/TLS)

flashcards.front: POP3 ports
flashcards.back: 110 (unencrypted), 995 (POP3 over SSL/TLS)

flashcards.front: SMTP ports
flashcards.back: 25 (server-to-server), 465 (implicit SSL), 587 (submission/STARTTLS — modern client send)

flashcards.front: What does Exchange ActiveSync do?
flashcards.back: Syncs email, calendar, and contacts and lets admins enforce policy (passcode, encryption, remote wipe)

flashcards.front: What is OAuth in mobile email setup?
flashcards.back: Token-based authorization; the app gets a revocable access token instead of storing the password (supports MFA)

flashcards.front: What does S/MIME provide for email?
flashcards.back: Certificate-based digital signing (authenticity/integrity) and encryption (confidentiality)

flashcards.front: MDM vs MAM
flashcards.back: MDM manages the entire device (enroll, policy, wipe); MAM manages only corporate apps/data (good for BYOD)

flashcards.front: BYOD vs COPE
flashcards.back: BYOD = employee-owned (cheaper, privacy concerns); COPE = corporate-owned, personally enabled (full IT control)

flashcards.front: What is full-device encryption for?
flashcards.back: Encrypts data-at-rest so a lost/stolen device's storage is unreadable without the credential

flashcards.front: Why is sideloading/jailbreaking risky?
flashcards.back: It bypasses app-store vetting and the OS sandbox, greatly increasing malware risk; usually banned by policy

flashcards.front: What does airplane mode disable?
flashcards.back: All radios — cellular, Wi-Fi, and Bluetooth (Wi-Fi/BT can be re-enabled individually)

flashcards.front: First step for a mobile connectivity problem
flashcards.back: Toggle airplane mode on/off to force the radios to re-register, then reboot

flashcards.front: Best action when a managed device is lost
flashcards.back: Remote-lock/locate first, then remote-wipe if recovery is hopeless — protecting data outranks hardware
